summary_one_line: Input must register multiple distinct hash names colliding into one bucket so the chain-walk sees insert->next != NULL.

hypotheses.what_input_feature: An XML document that registers at least two distinct named entries (name/name2/name3 triples) in xmlHashAddEntry3 whose computed key collides into the same bucket, so the bucket already holds a linked chain (table->table[key] has a non-NULL ->next) when a later entry is added.
hypotheses.why_winner_satisfies: cmplog resolves the branch in 8/10 trials with avg hitcount 1 on the branch; its I2SRandReplace mutator substitutes logged comparison operands from the chain-walk equality checks (insert->name == name) back into input bytes, producing several distinct-yet-related names that accumulate in one bucket and grow the ->next chain so the for-loop guard's true side is taken.
hypotheses.why_loser_doesnt: naive resolves only 1/10 trials with avg hitcount 0; its havoc/token mutators flip bytes blindly and tend to produce a single distinct name or no collision, so the bucket stays empty (insert == NULL via valid == 0) or holds exactly one entry with ->next == NULL, leaving the true branch untaken.
hypotheses.mechanism_attribution: cmplog's I2SRandReplace reads the CmpLogObserver buffer recorded at the name-equality comparisons in the chain-walk and splices a matched operand into the input at the offset where it appeared, generating extra hash entries that share a bucket; this comparison-introspection capability is exactly what naive lacks, explaining the cmplog>naive divergence.

evidence_trail.claim: The blocked true side is the loop guard insert->next != NULL inside xmlHashAddEntry3, requiring an existing chain in the bucket.
Entering the chain-walk loop requires the bucket to be already valid (non-empty), so a prior collision must have populated it.
The chain-walk compares insert->name/name2/name3 against the new name triple, the comparisons whose operands cmplog logs.
cmplog wins the branch 8/10 while naive blocks 9/10, with cmplog showing avg hitcount 1 vs naive 0.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: avg hitcount on branch: winner=1  loser=0

evidence_trail.claim: naive's mutators are blind byte/havoc/token operations with no comparison introspection.
evidence_trail.exact_quote: **Observed `mutation_op` in seed metadata**: any of the above. No I2S.

evidence_trail.claim: cmplog's I2SRandReplace substitutes a logged comparison operand into the input at matching offsets, the mechanism enabling distinct colliding names.
evidence_trail.exact_quote:
`I2SRandReplace` reads the post-execution `CmpLogObserver` buffer,
picks a CMP entry, scans the input for byte sequences matching one
operand, and substitutes the other operand at those offsets.

mechanism_consistency_check.verification_method: Ran `python3 tools/db_query.py more-seeds --branch 6402 --role W --limit 20` and `python3 tools/db_query.py lineage --branch 6402 --role W --fuzzer cmplog --trial 1 --seed any`; both returned no winner-resolving rows ('[no seeds in resolving_seeds matching filter]', '[no lineage rows in resolving_seed_lineage]'). The resolving seed for this hash-collision branch was unrecoverable due to cross-seed accumulation, so no I2S-floor (dash-row) check is possible. I2S contribution remains plausible but unverified; it could also exist in leaked havoc-bucket finds.

falsifiability.would_be_refuted_by: A synthetic harness requiring only a single distinct hash entry (no second colliding key) where cmplog still resolves the insert->next != NULL true side at the same rate as naive would refute the collision-chain requirement.

weakest_evidence_point: No winner-resolving seed or lineage is available, so the claim that I2SRandReplace specifically (rather than lucky havoc) generates the colliding names is inferred from source + counts, not directly observed.
